Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service — delivering only the most relevant documentation context to the client’s own LLM so large prompts fit token limits and queries are fast and accurate.

Key points:
- ~90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content)
- Returns OPTIMIZED CONTEXT (not an LLM response) for the client to feed into their LLM
- Client uses their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in, no LLM costs passed through)
- High margins (compute-focused) and LLM‑agnostic (works with any MCP‑compatible client)

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service — i.e., delivering only the small, highly relevant context your LLM needs so clients get better answers at far lower token cost. Key points:

- ~90% context reduction: compress large doc sets (e.g., 500K tokens) to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content.  
- Client uses their own LLM: no vendor lock‑in and no LLM costs passed through by Snipara.  
- High margins: product is pure compute/contexting, not LLM inference.  
- LLM‑agnostic: works with any MCP‑compatible client (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.).  

Key insight: the real value is the optimized context you feed your LLM — not the LLM’s response itself.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service:

- Reduce client documentation down to the most relevant context (≈90% token reduction).  
- Deliver optimized context so the client can use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in and no LLM costs passed through).  
- High-margin, compute-only business model.  
- LLM‑agnostic — works with any MCP‑compatible client.

High-level: Snipara is split across three repositories with a single production deploy. Each repo has a distinct role (web app + MCP server, PyPI client, and enterprise/self‑hosted mirror) and certain files/schemas must be kept in sync to avoid downtime or data loss.

1) alopez3006/snipara-webapp (monorepo) — primary production source
- Role: Main monorepo used for production.
- Key parts:
  - apps/web/        → Next.js 14 web application (frontend)
  - apps/mcp-server/ → Python FastAPI MCP server (api.snipara.com)
  - packages/database/ → JS Prisma schema and client (source of truth for DB models)
- Deployment: This is the repo that is deployed to Railway for production. Production MCP service: Railway service snipara-fastapi, endpoint api.snipara.com/mcp/<project-slug>.
- Notes: Production Dockerfile for apps/mcp-server does NOT run prisma db push (migrations are applied from the JS side).

2) alopez3006/snipara-mcp (standalone git, NOT deployed)
- Role: PyPI client package only — distribution package for the MCP client.
- Status: Separate repo used for packaging/publishing the snipara-mcp client to PyPI. Not used for production server deploys.

3) Snipara/snipara-server (enterprise / self-hosted mirror, NOT production)
- Role: Enterprise / self-hosted codebase; local clone used for on‑prem or enterprise customers.
- Key details:
  - Local path example: /Users/alopez/Devs/snipara-fastapi
  - Contains init-db.sh that runs prisma db push --accept-data-loss (dangerous if schemas diverge).
- Syncing: Changes made in the monorepo’s apps/mcp-server must be manually copied into this repo so enterprise builds stay current.

Critical operational points (why the split matters)
- Prisma schema sync is critical: apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma (Python) MUST be kept in sync with packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma (JS). If they drift, the enterprise init script can DROP columns (accept-data-loss), and NextAuth or other services can break.
- Files that must be synced to the enterprise repo after changes: server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma.
- Deployment hygiene:
  - Production deploy command (from monorepo): cd apps/mcp-server && railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"
  - After JS Prisma schema change: pnpm db:push (apply JS schema to Neon DB) → then redeploy the MCP server with synced Python schema.

Common failure modes to watch for
- 500s on MCP calls when config/settings fields are missing in Python Settings (ensure new settings are added).
- Missing DB columns / NextAuth crashes from stale Python schema + enterprise init-db.sh.
- Device flow POSTs failing due to 301 redirects if using bare snipara.com instead of www.snipara.com.

Summary
- Monorepo (webapp) = production source (frontend + production MCP server) + JS Prisma schema (source of truth).
- PyPI repo = client packaging only.
- Enterprise repo = self‑hosted mirror that must be manually synced and carefully managed to avoid destructive schema pushes.

Snipara’s deployment/operational architecture is split across three repositories, each with a distinct role:

1) alopez3006/snipara-webapp (monorepo) — primary production source
- Contains the web app and the MCP server used in production:
  - apps/web — Next.js 14 web application (frontend)
  - apps/mcp-server — Python FastAPI MCP server (production service deployed to Railway as snipara-fastapi)
  - packages/database — Prisma JS schema and client (source-of-truth DB models)
- This monorepo is what’s actually deployed to api.snipara.com (Railway service snipara-fastapi).

2) alopez3006/snipara-mcp (standalone)
- A separate git repo that hosts the PyPI client package only (snipara-mcp).
- Not deployed as a service; used to publish the Python MCP client to PyPI.

3) Snipara/snipara-server (enterprise / self‑hosted mirror)
- Enterprise/self-hosted repo containing a local clone of the MCP server code used by customers who self-host.
- Includes tooling/scripts (e.g., init-db.sh) and runs a Python Prisma schema that can run prisma db push --accept-data-loss (dangerous if out of sync).
- NOT used for the public production deploy, but must be kept in sync when releasing enterprise builds.

How they interact and the important operational details
- Production runs from the monorepo’s apps/mcp-server (Railway service snipara-fastapi → api.snipara.com).
- The JS Prisma schema (packages/database) is considered the canonical schema; the Python Prisma schema in apps/mcp-server must be kept in sync with it because both point at the same Neon PostgreSQL database.
- The enterprise repo also has a Python Prisma schema; if it drifts and its init scripts are used, running prisma db push --accept-data-loss can DROP columns.
- The monorepo production Dockerfile DOES NOT run prisma db push; schema changes are applied via pnpm db:push (JS side) then deploy the synced Python schema.

Key commands / sync actions
- Deploy MCP server from monorepo:
  cd apps/mcp-server
  railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"
- After JS Prisma schema changes:
  pnpm db:push
  cd apps/mcp-server
  railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"
- Sync changes to enterprise repo when needed by copying files (server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma) into the enterprise repo and committing.

Risks and mitigations
- Schema drift → Data loss: Always sync the Python Prisma schema with the JS schema before any enterprise init scripts are run. Never run enterprise init-db.sh against production without verifying schemas.
- MCP 500 errors from missing Settings fields: Grep for settings references when adding features; ensure Settings contains every used attribute.
- Device flow 405: Use www.snipara.com for POST endpoints (bare snipara.com 301s → GET).
- Post-deploy checks: curl https://api.snipara.com/health and call MCP tools/list and tools/call to validate licensing, DB, and engine paths; check Railway logs for errors.

In short: production is served from a single monorepo (web + MCP server + JS Prisma schema), the PyPI client lives in a separate repo, and an enterprise mirror repo exists for self-hosted customers — keeping Prisma schemas and key server files synchronized across these three components is critical to avoid outages or data loss.

Snipara’s three-component architecture is split across three repositories with distinct roles and deployment targets:

1. Monorepo (alopez3006/snipara-webapp)
- Purpose: Primary source-of-truth and production deploy.
- Contains:
  - apps/web — Next.js 14 frontend (deployed to Railway as snipara-webapp).
  - apps/mcp-server — Python FastAPI MCP server (deployed to Railway as snipara-fastapi; powers api.snipara.com/mcp/<project>).
  - packages/database — JS Prisma schema & client (the canonical JS DB schema).
- Notes: This repo is used for production images and contains the Dockerfile used for deploy.

2. PyPI client repo (alopez3006/snipara-mcp)

- Not deployed as a server; used as a library/client.

3. Enterprise / self-hosted repo (Snipara/snipara-server)

- Important: Contains an init-db.sh that runs prisma db push --accept-data-loss (dangerous if schemas diverge). Must be kept in sync with monorepo changes.

Production deploy summary
- api.snipara.com is built from apps/mcp-server in the monorepo and deployed to Railway service snipara-fastapi.

Critical operational points / risks
- Prisma schema must be kept in sync between JS (packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma) and Python (apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma). If they drift, enterprise init scripts can drop DB columns (prisma db push --accept-data-loss).
- After JS schema changes: run pnpm db:push, sync Python schema, then redeploy the MCP server.
- Files that must be synced to the enterprise repo after changes: server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma.

That is the high-level three-part architecture and the key deployment/sync responsibilities to keep production and enterprise installs safe.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a fixed token budget you set (or use the tool defaults) and returns an OPTIMIZED context that fits inside that budget by searching, ranking, chunking and compressing documentation. You pass max_tokens (or use the preset for the tool) and Snipara allocates that budget across sources, returns only the most relevant sections, and compresses them to fit.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. You call a Snipara MCP tool with a token budget (examples: rlm_ask ≈ 2.5K tokens; rlm_context_query with max_tokens you choose — common LITE budgets ~3–5K; FULL workflows ~8–15K).
2. Snipara performs search + semantic ranking (keyword/semantic/hybrid), scores candidate sections, and picks the highest‑value sections.
3. It compresses/chunks those sections (summaries, truncation, smart stitching) so the final returned context fits the requested token budget.
4. If team shared context is enabled, Snipara includes shared docs automatically and divides the budget between project docs and shared context according to configured rules.
5. The tool returns OPTIMIZED CONTEXT (not an LLM answer) for your LLM to consume.

Key budgeting rules and defaults
- LITE vs FULL:
  - LITE mode (common): target budget ~3–5K tokens (use for quick edits, small features).
  - FULL mode (complex): target budget ~8–15K tokens (multi‑file/architectural work).
- Tool defaults:
  - rlm_ask: quick/simple, ~2500 tokens.
  - rlm_context_query: full feature, configurable max_tokens (examples use 6K, 8K, etc.).
  - rlm_multi_project_query and rlm_plan can request larger budgets (examples show 8K).
- Shared context allocation:
  - Shared categories are allocated percentages of the shared budget: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - When project + shared context are combined, Snipara distributes budget (example: 60% project docs + 40% shared context).
  - rlm_shared_context responses include total_tokens and budget_allocation so you can inspect the split.

How you control budgeting
- Set max_tokens on rlm_context_query / rlm_multi_project_query / rlm_plan.
- Choose search_mode/strategy (e.g., hybrid, relevance_first, depth_first) to bias results.
- Use per_project_limit on multi_project queries to cap sections per project.
- Use rlm_plan/rlm_decompose to get suggested budget allocations per implementation step.
- Use rlm_shared_context to fetch team docs and see the exact token allocation returned.

Practical guidance
- For quick lookups use rlm_ask (~2.5K). For implementation chunks use rlm_context_query with ~6K.
- For full feature planning start with rlm_plan and request a larger max_tokens (8–15K).
- Rely on Snipara to compress/context‑optimize; you pass the returned context to your LLM (Snipara does not do the final inference).
- If Snipara tools error, fall back to local reads/grep per the non‑negotiable rules.

Examples from the docs
- rlm_context_query(query="authentication", max_tokens=6000, search_mode="hybrid")
- rlm_shared_context(...) returns:
  - "total_tokens": 2847
  - "budget_allocation": { "MANDATORY": 1139, "BEST_PRACTICES": 854, "GUIDELINES": 569, "REFERENCE": 285 }
- LITE mode workflow: rlm_context_query("task") → Read files → Edit → pnpm test (token budget ~3–5K)
- FULL mode workflow: phasing + rlm_plan + rlm_decompose + rlm_context_query(max_tokens=8000) (token budget ~8–15K)

Why it matters
- The budgeting system is the core of RLM: it reduces huge docsets (50K+ tokens) down to the few thousand tokens your LLM can handle reliably (the docs cite ~90% context reduction), while preserving the most relevant information and enforcing team standards when shared context is included.

If you want, I can:
- Recommend specific max_tokens for a given task size,
- Show an example rlm_context_query call tuned for your repo,
- Or explain how to inspect the returned token counts from rlm_shared_context / rlm_context_query responses.

Short answer
Snipara enforces a strict "context budgeting" model: you tell Snipara how many tokens you want returned (via max_tokens), and the MCP pipeline (search → rank → score → compress/chunk) returns an optimized, token-budgeted bundle of documentation/context you can paste into your LLM. The goal is to deliver only the most relevant context that fits the client LLM's token limits.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
- Query + budget: Calls like rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=...) or rlm_plan(max_tokens=...) let you specify the token budget you expect the result to fit.
- Search & rank: Snipara semantically searches the indexed docs, ranks sections by relevance, and scores them for importance relative to the query.
- Compress & chunk: Selected sections are compressed, trimmed, and chunked so the final returned context respects your max_tokens budget.
- Return: Snipara returns OPTIMIZED CONTEXT (not an LLM answer) — you pass that context to your own LLM for inference.

Key budgeting rules & defaults
- rlm_ask is a quick/simple tool with a predictable budget (~2,500 tokens) and no extra config — use for fast lookups.
- rlm_context_query is full-featured and accepts max_tokens (common values shown below).
- LITE mode (90% of tasks): target ~3–5K tokens for context (fast, single-session work).
- FULL mode (complex features): target ~8–15K tokens for multi-chunk, multi-session work.
- Typical efficiency: Snipara aims for ~90% context reduction (e.g., from 50K+ source tokens down to ~5–6K useful tokens).

Shared context and allocation
- When team/shared context is included, Snipara automatically allocates budget between project docs and shared collections. Example: 60% project docs + 40% shared context.
- Shared collections themselves are budgeted by category by default: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%. rlm_shared_context(...) returns a budget_allocation object you can inspect.

Tools that help manage budgets
- rlm_plan(...) can return suggested token budgets per step when generating execution plans.
- rlm_decompose(...) breaks tasks into chunks so you can request per-chunk budgets (smaller, focused contexts).
- rlm_multi_project_query(...) accepts max_tokens and per_project_limit to control token spend across many projects.

Practical examples
- Quick lookup:
  rlm_ask("How does authentication work?")
  → ~2.5K tokens returned
- Chunked implementation step:
  context = rlm_context_query(query="implement token refresh", max_tokens=6000, search_mode="hybrid")
  → returns up to ~6K tokens of optimized context for that chunk
- Full plan:
  plan = rlm_plan(query="Implement OAuth", max_tokens=16000, strategy="relevance_first")
  → plan suggests budgets for each sub-step

Best practices
- Pick budget based on complexity: 3–5K for small fixes, 8–15K for multi-file or architectural work.
- Use rlm_plan and rlm_decompose to get finer-grained budget recommendations for multi-step work.
- Include shared context intentionally (MANDATORY rules should always be included) — Snipara will reserve part of the budget accordingly.
- If Snipara errors (500/timeout), fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob — but always try Snipara first.
- Keep budgeted contexts small and focused per chunk to maximize relevance and LLM performance.

Bottom line
Token budgeting is explicit and configurable: you control max_tokens, Snipara chooses the most relevant content and compresses it to fit, and shared/team rules are automatically allocated within that budget using the documented percentages. Use rlm_context_query/rlm_plan/rlm_decompose to manage budgets for multi-step work, and use rlm_ask for fast, predictable lookups.

Short summary
- Snipara never returns an LLM reply — it returns an OPTIMIZED CONTEXT sized to your token budget. You tell Snipara how many tokens you want (or use defaults) and it searches, ranks, compresses and chunks documentation to fit that budget.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Caller specifies a token budget (explicitly via rlm_context_query.max_tokens or implicitly by choosing a tool). rlm_ask is a small, predictable budget (~2.5K); rlm_context_query is configurable (common LITE ≈ 3–5K, FULL ≈ 8–15K).
2. Snipara does semantic/keyword search + ranking to find the most relevant sections across project docs (and any shared context).
3. It allocates the token budget across sources and categories using priority rules (e.g., project docs vs shared context; within shared context the category split example is MANDATORY 40% / BEST_PRACTICES 30% / GUIDELINES 20% / REFERENCE 10%).
4. Top ranked sections are compressed/chunked (summarized, trimmed, or concatenated) to meet the per-query max_tokens constraint while preserving the highest-value content.
5. The service returns the optimized context (sections list, tokens used, and often a budget_allocation breakdown) which you then pass to your LLM.

Key defaults and examples
- rlm_ask: quick/simple, ~2500 token budget, no config needed.
- LITE mode (most tasks): use rlm_context_query with ~3–5K token budget.
- FULL mode (complex/multi-session): use larger budgets (8–15K) and the full workflow (rlm_plan, rlm_decompose, rlm_context_query).
- Shared context allocation: Snipara automatically includes team/shared docs and distributes budget (example: 60% project docs + 40% shared context; shared context itself split MANDATORY 40% / BEST_PRACTICES 30% / GUIDELINES 20% / REFERENCE 10%).
- Multi-project queries: you can set per_project_limit and a total max_tokens for rlm_multi_project_query.

Tools that influence budgeting
- rlm_context_query: full-featured, max_tokens parameter, hybrid search modes, returns token usage and sections.
- rlm_ask: fast, predictable small budget.
- rlm_plan / rlm_decompose: suggest per-step token budgets for multi-step implementations.
- rlm_shared_context: fetches team docs with calculated budget_allocation.

Practical tips
- Choose LITE vs FULL first (use scoring rules in WORKFLOW_MODES.md) to pick a sensible budget.
- Use rlm_ask for quick lookups; use rlm_context_query when you need larger, curated context.
- Pre-load team standards with rlm_shared_context if you want them included consistently — Snipara will reserve the configured portion of the budget for those docs.
- Inspect the returned token counts/budget_allocation to tune max_tokens if you need more or less context.

Why this matters
- Token budgeting enables the 90% context reduction promise: Snipara delivers only the highest-value content (e.g., ~5–6K tokens instead of 50K+), so your LLM can answer accurately within its token limits.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-wide documentation bundles (coding standards, best practices, templates, guidelines) that projects automatically inherit so every developer and RLM query can use the same rules and references.

How they work (step-by-step)
1. Create a collection and upload documents (coding standards, templates, etc.) using rlm_upload_shared_document or the dashboard. Teams can list collections via rlm_list_collections (Team plan required to see team collections).
2. Link the collection to one or more projects in the dashboard. Linked projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
3. When you query project context (rlm_context_query), Snipara automatically includes shared context in results and splits the token budget between project docs and shared context (example default: 60% project / 40% shared).
4. You can explicitly fetch team standards up front with rlm_shared_context and inject them into your session (rlm_inject) so every subsequent query/implementation step uses the same rules.
5. Use rlm_get_template / rlm_list_templates to fetch team prompt templates stored in shared collections.

Document categories and token budgeting
- Categories (priority order): MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE.
- Typical token budget allocation example: MANDATORY = 40%, BEST_PRACTICES = 30%, GUIDELINES = 20%, REFERENCE = 10%. Snipara distributes context tokens across project vs shared docs according to configuration when returning rlm_context_query results.

Tools / API surface (common calls)

Short answer — Snipara supports OAuth device‑flow tokens for MCP access. Key points:

- Header format: use the standard Authorization bearer header:
  - Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_<token>
  - (Device‑flow tokens are represented as snipara_at_... tokens per the MCP quick reference.)
- Use case: device flow authentication (for devices or CLIs where interactive OAuth in a browser is required).
- Common failure to avoid: never POST to https://api.snipara.com (bare snipara.com) — that host 301‑redirects to www.snipara.com and a POST can be converted to GET (causes a silent device‑flow failure / 405). Always use the www host for API POST endpoints (https://www.snipara.com or the documented API hostname).
- Troubleshooting:
  - If you see "OAuth expired" → re-run the device flow to obtain a fresh snipara_at_ token.
  - If you see "Invalid API key" → generate a new key in the dashboard.
- For full device‑flow steps and alternatives (API keys, team keys, rate limits), see docs/AUTHENTICATION.md in the repo.

How it works (summary)
- Snipara supports OAuth device-flow authentication for clients that can’t easily open a browser (CLI, devices, etc.). The result is an OAuth access token you use to call Snipara APIs.
- Token header format (Snipara): Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_<token>
- If an OAuth token expires, the documented remediation is to re-authenticate using the device flow.

Typical device-flow steps (what you’ll do)
1. Start the device flow from your client (CLI or app). Snipara will return a verification URL and a user code (standard device-flow behavior).
2. The end user opens the verification URL (on a browser-capable device), enters the user code, and approves the app.
3. Your client polls Snipara’s token endpoint until the user completes authorization and an access token is issued.
4. Use the issued access token in requests:
   - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_<token>
   - Calls that require auth then succeed using that header.

Snipara-specific tips & gotchas (from the docs)
- Always use www.snipara.com for API POST endpoints. Using snipara.com can 301-redirect to www.snipara.com, which converts POST→GET per HTTP rules and can cause the device flow to fail with a 405. In short: never POST to bare snipara.com; use www.snipara.com.
- If you see “OAuth expired”, re-run the device flow to get a fresh token.
- For MCP access you can also use project/team API keys (X-API-Key: rlm_...) — those are distinct from OAuth tokens. Use Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... for OAuth device-flow tokens.
- If you get “Invalid API key” errors for key-based auth, generate a new key in the dashboard; for OAuth errors re-authenticate.

Quick example (header)
- Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_012345abcdef...

Where to look next
- See the repo docs for full authentication reference: docs/AUTHENTICATION.md and docs/AUTH_REFERENCE.md. For MCP-specific usage and debugging, check the MCP Authentication Quick Reference in the project docs.

Short summary
- Snipara supports standard OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization (Device) Flow for authenticating devices/CLI/embedded clients that can’t show a browser.
- The client starts the flow, the user completes authorization in a browser (or phone), the client polls the token endpoint and receives an access token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...).
- Use the returned token on subsequent API calls in the Authorization header.

Step‑by‑step (typical Device Flow)
1. Client requests a device authorization from Snipara’s device endpoint. Server returns:
   - device_code (for polling)
   - user_code (shown to the user)
   - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
   - expires_in and recommended polling interval
2. Show the user the user_code and verification_uri (or a QR). The user visits the verification URI and signs in/approves the client.
3. Client polls the token endpoint using device_code at the recommended interval.
4. When the user approves, the token endpoint returns an access token (and often a refresh token). Snipara access tokens use the Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... form.
5. Use that access token in API calls:
   - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
6. When the token expires, either use the refresh token (if provided) or repeat the device flow.

Snipara-specific notes & gotchas
- Always use the fully qualified API host for POST endpoints: use www.snipara.com (or the documented api host) — using bare snipara.com can 301-redirect to www.snipara.com and convert POST→GET, causing a silent 405 failure for device flow POSTs.
- Token header format: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Respect the server-provided polling interval when polling the token endpoint to avoid rate-limit or throttling errors.
- For full details (exact endpoints, request/response bodies, expiry/refresh behavior and rate limits) see docs/AUTHENTICATION.md in the repository.

Troubleshooting quick checks
- If you get 405 on device token POSTs → check you’re POSTing to the correct host (www.snipara.com) and correct endpoint path.
- If polling never succeeds → ensure user completed verification_uri flow and client is polling with the correct device_code and interval.
- If “Invalid token” errors → confirm you’re sending Authorization: Bearer <token> and the token hasn’t expired.

Short answer — the Snipara data model centers around teams, projects, content collections, and auth/usage. The main database models (conceptual) are:

- User  
  - Accounts for people who sign in (NextAuth integration). Tied to teams, API keys, sessions.

- Team (Organization)  
  - Groups users, owns projects, team-level API keys, and shared collections.

- Project  
  - Per-repository/project scope (e.g., `snipara`, `vibe-coding`). Has MCP config (.mcp.json), indexed docs, and project-level settings.

- APIKey / Credential  
  - Project and team API keys used for MCP authentication (X-API-Key). Tracks key metadata and permissions.

- License / Plan  
  - Billing/plan info that gates features (Context plans, Agent plans) and is used by license resolution logic.

- Collection (Shared Context Collection)  
  - A named collection of shared documents (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Linked to teams/projects.

- Document  
  - An uploaded or indexed document (feature spec, coding standard, README). Fields include title, path, content, category, priority, collection_id.

- Section / ContextSection  
  - Chunked/indexed pieces of a document used for semantic search and context queries (returned by rlm_context_query / rlm_stats).

- Template  
  - Prompt templates stored in collections (used by rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template).

- Remember / Memory / SessionEntry  
  - Saved session context, decisions, learnings (rlm_remember / rlm_recall). Used for resumable sessions.

- Usage / Stats  
  - Query/usage telemetry (rlm_stats) for rate-limiting, billing, and analytics.

- Multi-project link / ProjectCollectionLink  
  - Links that attach shared collections to projects (so projects inherit team standards).

- Deployment / MCPServer (operational model)  
  - Records MCP server endpoints, deploy metadata, and health status for api.snipara.com and project endpoints.

- JS Prisma schema: packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma  
- Python Prisma schema (MCP server): apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma  
- Python server models: apps/mcp-server/src/models.py

Important operational note
- The two Prisma schemas must be kept in sync (JS ↔ Python). See the “Prisma Schema Sync (CRITICAL)” section in the docs.

Short answer
- Snipara (RLM) reduces a huge codebase down to a small, highly relevant context payload (typical ~5K tokens) that you pass to your LLM, so you never need to feed the model hundreds of thousands of tokens. That makes the 128K window trivial to satisfy and massively cuts token costs, latency, and noise.

How Snipara helps (what it does)
1. Index your 500K-token repo (markdown, code, docs).
2. On each question, run semantic search + ranking to find only the relevant sections.
3. Compress / chunk / prioritize and produce an OPTIMIZED CONTEXT payload sized to your LLM budget (e.g., ~5K tokens).
4. Return that context (you send it to your own LLM). Snipara charges for the context query only — it does not run LLM inference for you.

Cost / savings — concrete example
Assumptions (example numbers; replace with your LLM pricing):
- Snipara returns ~5K tokens of context (typical claim).
- Your LLM has a 128K window; you might be tempted to include many more tokens (up to 128K or more).
- Example LLM input price: $0.03 per 1K input tokens.

Costs per call:
- Sending 128K tokens to LLM: 128 * $0.03 = $3.84
- Sending 5K tokens (Snipara-optimized): 5 * $0.03 = $0.15
- Token cost saved per call: $3.84 − $0.15 = $3.69 (≈96% savings vs using full 128K)
- Versus sending whole 500K tokens: 500 * $0.03 = $15 → savings ≈ $14.85 (≈99%)

Snipara service cost example (from docs)
- PRO plan: $19/mo for 5,000 context queries → ≈ $0.0038 per query.
- Net saving per query (using examples above): $3.69 − $0.0038 ≈ $3.686 → ~970× ROI on the Snipara per-query cost (your actual ROI depends on your LLM prices and Snipara plan).

Other benefits beyond raw token cost
- Much lower latency and faster responses (smaller context).
- Better model accuracy (less irrelevant context / reduced hallucination risk).
- Consistent token budgeting across queries (can target LITE vs FULL workflows).
- Ability to make more queries per month within your LLM quota (or reduce your LLM spending substantially).

Caveats / notes
- Exact savings scale with your LLM provider/pricing and the context size Snipara returns (you can tune max_tokens per query).
- Snipara charges for context optimization queries (plans in docs); compare that to your LLM per-token costs to compute ROI for your usage pattern.
- For very cheap or self-hosted LLMs, financial savings shrink but you still gain accuracy, latency, and developer productivity benefits.

Short answer
- Snipara (RLM/Snipara) searches, ranks, and compresses your 500K‑token codebase into a small, highly relevant context that fits your LLM’s window (e.g., ~3–15K tokens depending on mode). The platform’s core claim: ~90%+ context reduction (500K → ~5K tokens of relevant content).
- That means you no longer try to shove the whole repo into the model — you pass the optimized context Snipara returns to your LLM. You pay Snipara for the context optimization query; you don’t pay Snipara for LLM inference. That yields very large token-cost savings on your LLM provider.

How it helps (step by step)
1. Search & rank: rlm_context_query performs semantic search across the 500K tokens, finds the most relevant sections, and scores them.
2. Budgeted compression/chunking: Snipara trims, compresses, and chunks those sections to match the token budget you choose (LITE ≈ 3–5K tokens; FULL ≈ 6–15K tokens).
3. Return optimized context: You get a small, high‑signal context payload (not an LLM answer) to feed into your own LLM.
4. LLM does the reasoning: Your LLM uses only that optimized context (plus the prompt), so you stay within your 128K window with margin to spare.

Concrete cost-savings example (hypothetical)
- Reduction: 500K → ~5K = 100× fewer tokens included in context (or 500K → 15K in FULL mode = ~33× reduction).
- If your LLM billed $0.02 per 1K input tokens (example only):
  - Sending 128K tokens: 128 × $0.02 = $2.56 per call
  - Sending 5K tokens: 5 × $0.02 = $0.10 per call
  - Savings: $2.46 per call ≈ 96% reduction in LLM input cost
- Real savings depend on your LLM pricing and the final token budget you choose, but you should expect an order‑of‑magnitude (10×–100×) reduction in tokens sent to the model for typical queries.

Other benefits
- Faster responses and lower latency (smaller context to tokenize and process).
- Predictable token usage (use LITE vs FULL budgets).
- Team/shared context is included automatically and budgeted (e.g., 40% shared + 60% project docs) so standards aren’t lost.
- No vendor lock‑in: you keep using your own LLM and its pricing model.

How to use it (quick)
- For a single query: rlm_context_query(query="<your question>", max_tokens=5000) — take the returned context and pass it to your LLM.
- Use LITE mode for quick/small tasks (~3–5K tokens) and FULL mode for complex work (~6–15K tokens).

Bottom line
Snipara converts an unwieldy 500K token codebase into a tiny, high‑relevance context that fits easily inside a 128K window. That reduces the tokens you send to your LLM by an order of magnitude or more, yielding large (typically 90%+ relative) reductions in LLM token costs while giving you more accurate, focused prompts.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query when you need relevant, budgeted context for a specific question or implementation step.
- Use rlm_decompose when the task/query itself is complex and should be split into smaller sub‑tasks (chunks) with dependencies before you fetch context or start implementing.

When to pick which (details)
- rlm_context_query
  - Purpose: semantic search + token budgeting → returns the optimized context sections you pass to your LLM or the runtime.
  - Use it for: documentation/code lookups, answering “How does X work?”, or for a single implementation chunk. (This is the FIRST tool to try for normal documentation queries per the NON‑NEGOTIABLE rules.)
  - Typical LITE usage: quick fixes or single‑chunk work (token budgets ~3–5K).
  - Example: context = rlm_context_query(query="implement user registration endpoint", max_tokens=6000, search_mode="hybrid")

- rlm_decompose
  - Purpose: split a complex query/feature into sub‑queries, return sub‑tasks, dependencies, and suggested execution order and budgets.
  - Use it for: multi‑file features, architectural work, multi‑session projects — i.e., when the task must be planned and executed chunk‑by‑chunk (FULL mode).
  - Typical FULL workflow: rlm_plan → rlm_decompose → for each chunk call rlm_context_query → implement & test.
  - Example: chunks = rlm_decompose(query="OAuth 2.0 integration implementation", max_depth=2) → yields Chunk 1..N with dependencies.

How they work together (recommended workflow)
1. If the user asked a straightforward doc/code question → call rlm_context_query first.
2. If the task is complex (multi‑file, architectural, multi‑session) → run rlm_plan (optional) then rlm_decompose to get chunks.
3. For each chunk from rlm_decompose, call rlm_context_query to fetch optimized context for implementation/testing.
4. Use RLM‑Runtime or your LLM with that returned context to implement/test, then rlm_remember/rlm_store_summary.

Quick heuristic (from docs)
- Score >= 2 on the QUICK DECISION checklist → FULL mode → use rlm_decompose.
- Otherwise → LITE mode → rlm_context_query is usually sufficient.

Tips
- Use rlm_ask for very quick, small queries (~2500 tokens) when you don't need configuration.
- Set sensible max_tokens per chunk (docs recommend ~6K per chunk during iterative implementation).
- Always try Snipara MCP (rlm_context_query) first for simple doc lookups; decompose only when planning/execution complexity warrants it.

- Use rlm_decompose when the work itself is complex and you need to break it into smaller, ordered sub‑tasks (chunks) before you start retrieving context and implementing.

When to pick which (rules of thumb)
- rlm_context_query
  - Quick lookups: “How does authentication work?”, “Where is the user model defined?”, or a single-file change.
  - LITE tasks (bug fixes, small features, known files).
  - Single-step implementation: get the ~3–6K tokens of context for a task and go code/test.
  - First call for documentation/code searches (per the non‑negotiable workflow). If it errors (500/timeout), fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob.

- rlm_decompose
  - FULL mode work: multi-day features, architectural decisions, or anything that scores 2+ on the QUICK DECISION rubric (multiple sessions, affects 5+ files, architectural impact, others will need to understand it later).
  - When you need an execution plan broken into chunks with dependencies, ordering, and suggested budgets.
  - Use after rlm_plan (or when you already know the high‑level task) to produce sub‑queries you will iterate on.

Typical workflow (recommended)
1. If question/task is simple → rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=3-6000, search_mode="hybrid") → implement.
2. If task is complex → rlm_plan(...) to get plan → rlm_decompose(query, max_depth=1–2) to get chunks + dependencies → for each chunk call rlm_context_query(chunk_query, max_tokens=~6000) → implement chunk-by-chunk (rlm-runtime, tests, rlm_remember, etc).

Examples
- “How is auth implemented across projects?” → start with rlm_context_query("authentication") or rlm_multi_project_query for cross‑project search.
- “Implement OAuth 2.0 integration from spec” → rlm_plan(...); rlm_decompose("OAuth 2.0 integration", max_depth=2) → iterate chunks and call rlm_context_query for each chunk.

Practical tips
- rlm_decompose controls granularity with max_depth; choose larger depth for more fine‑grained tasks.
- rlm_context_query controls token budget with max_tokens and search_mode ("hybrid" recommended).
- Always try rlm_context_query first for doc/code lookups. Use rlm_decompose when you need structure and repeatable chunked work.

Guidance (mode → when to use + examples)

- Hybrid (recommended default)
  - When: most documentation/code queries where you want both relevance and token efficiency.
  - Why: combines semantic understanding with keyword/term matching — finds conceptually relevant sections but favors exact matches when present.
  - Use with: rlm_context_query(search_mode="hybrid")
  - Example: "How does authentication work?" or "Implement OAuth integration in this project."

- Semantic
  - When: conceptual, exploratory, or “find similar/analogous” queries where wording varies across docs.
  - Why: finds conceptually related content even if different wording/identifiers are used.
  - Use with: rlm_context_query(search_mode="semantic")
  - Example: "Best practices for token rotation" or "Show examples of rate-limiting patterns."

- Keyword (literal)
  - When: you need exact matches (function/class names, config keys, error codes) or the query contains precise identifiers.
  - Why: finds exact occurrences quickly, less noise from related concepts.
  - Use with: rlm_context_query(search_mode="keyword") or rlm_search for pattern searches
  - Example: "Find occurrences of `createSession()`" or "Where is `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` referenced?"

- Regex / Pattern searches
  - When: you need structural or pattern-based matches (imports, TODO comments, config blocks).
  - Why: precise control over matching; ideal for code migrations or refactors.
  - Use with: rlm_search (regex) or local grep if you already know file paths
  - Example: `rlm_search(pattern="export function .*Auth", file_types=["ts"])` or local grep for specific file editing.

Short summary — available methods
- Project API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Use: single-project programmatic access
- Team API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Use: team-level programmatic access across all projects
- OAuth (device flow)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Use: interactive/device CLI login (device flow)
- End‑user web auth (NextAuth)
  - Providers: GitHub, Google, Email
  - Use: web app user sign-in and session management

How they differ in security (practical implications)
- Scope / blast radius
  - Team API Key > Project API Key — team keys grant access to all projects, so if leaked they have a much larger impact. Treat team keys as highly privileged.
- Lifespan & revocability
  - API keys are long‑lived bearer secrets (rotate/revoke manually). OAuth tokens (device flow) are short‑lived and designed to be revoked/rotated by token flows, making them safer for interactive/CLI usage.
- Identity & auditing
  - NextAuth / OAuth provides per-user identity (via provider accounts), better for auditing, user-based permissions, and leveraging provider security (MFA, SSO). API keys are service-level and don’t map to a human user.
- Ease of safe use
  - API keys are easy to misuse (checked into code, config leaks). OAuth/device flows encourage ephemeral tokens and interactive approval, reducing accidental exposure.
- Operational pitfalls called out in docs
  - Device flow POSTs can fail if you use the bare domain (snipara.com) because it 301→www and POST→GET; always use www.snipara.com for API POST endpoints.
  - If an API key becomes invalid, generate a new key in the dashboard; if an OAuth token expires, re‑authenticate via device flow.

Recommended best practices (from docs + security hygiene)
- Use the least‑privilege credential (project key instead of team key) unless broader access is required.
- Prefer OAuth/device flow for CLI/interactive access and for end‑user flows to get short‑lived, revocable tokens and user identity.
- Store keys securely (secrets manager), rotate regularly, and revoke compromised keys immediately.
- Don’t embed keys in public repos or client-side code.
- For POST requests and device flow calls, use www.snipara.com (avoid bare snipara.com redirects).
- If you see "Invalid API key" or OAuth expired errors, regenerate key or re‑authenticate per the dashboard/device flow.

Summary — available auth methods
- Project API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_<...>
  - Use: Single-project service access (per-project API key in .mcp.json)
- Team API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_<...>
  - Use: Team-level key granting access to multiple projects
- OAuth (device flow / bearer tokens)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_<...>
  - Use: User-level auth via OAuth/device flow (user session tokens)
- Web/interactive login via NextAuth (GitHub, Google, Email)
  - Use: Browser/web app user authentication and session management (NextAuth sessions / cookies)

How they differ in security (short comparison)
- Scope & Least Privilege
  - Project API Key: narrowest scope — limited to one project. Lower blast radius if leaked.
  - Team API Key: broader scope — can access many projects. Higher blast radius; treat as more sensitive.
  - OAuth bearer token: user-scoped; can be fine-grained via scopes/consent. Often better for per-user operations and auditing.
  - NextAuth sessions: intended for end-user logins in the web app; tied to identity providers (OAuth) and session cookies; supports refresh/expiry patterns.

- Lifespan & Revocation
  - Project / Team API Keys: typically long-lived until rotated/revoked. Easier to misuse if embedded in clients. Require explicit rotation to revoke.
  - OAuth tokens: usually short-lived (with refresh token flow available) and revocable via provider — better for temporary access.
  - NextAuth sessions: have configurable expiration and are revocable by server-side session invalidation.

- Exposure Risk & Usage Surface
  - API Keys (X-API-Key): high risk if embedded in client-side code or public repos. Must be stored server-side or in environment variables.
  - Team API Key: higher risk because of wider access — restrict storage/access tightly, use audit logs.
  - OAuth/device flow: lower risk for device/CLI flows because user authorizes interactively; tokens are bearer tokens but tend to be short-lived.
  - NextAuth: secure for browser flows when using httpOnly cookies and CSRF protections.

- Auditability & User Context
  - API Keys: less granular user identity (key maps to project/team), harder to attribute to a specific user.
  - OAuth / NextAuth: better user-level attribution and audit trails.

- Recommended use
  - Use Project API Keys for machine-to-machine calls scoped to a single project.
  - Use Team API Keys only when cross-project automation is required — protect and rotate frequently.
  - Use OAuth/device flow for CLI/device & user-driven authentication (preferable for per-user actions).
  - Use NextAuth (OAuth providers) for web app user sign-in and session management.

Best practices (from docs + general security)
- Always use HTTPS for API calls.
- Never embed API keys in client-side code or public repos — store in server env vars or secret manager.
- Prefer short-lived tokens (OAuth) where possible; rotate API keys regularly.
- Restrict keys by IP, origin, or scope if the platform supports it.
- Monitor & audit usage; revoke compromised keys immediately.
- For device flow POST endpoints, use the correct hostname: use www.snipara.com (not snipara.com) — otherwise POSTs can be redirected and fail (305/405).
- If you see "Invalid API key", generate a new key in the dashboard and update configs.

Short answer
- When shared context is included in a project query, the total token budget is split between project documents and the team shared context: by default 60% → project docs, 40% → shared context.
- The shared-context portion is then allocated across the four document categories using fixed default percentages:
  - MANDATORY: 40% of shared-context tokens
  - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
  - GUIDELINES: 20%
  - REFERENCE: 10%

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. You call rlm_context_query (or rlm_shared_context) with a max_tokens budget (e.g., 6,000).
2. Snipara splits that budget: 60% to project content, 40% to shared context (e.g., 3,600 project / 2,400 shared).
3. The shared 2,400 tokens are distributed to categories per the default percentages above:
   - MANDATORY = 40% of 2,400 = 960 tokens
   - BEST_PRACTICES = 30% = 720 tokens
   - GUIDELINES = 20% = 480 tokens
   - REFERENCE = 10% = 240 tokens
4. Within each category, documents are ranked by their priority (0–100) and by relevance; Snipara picks and compresses/truncates sections to fit each category’s allocated token budget.
5. The rlm_shared_context() response includes a budget_allocation map showing the exact token splits for the request.

Notes and caveats
- Priority (0–100) affects which documents within a category are included first; higher priority = more likely to be included.
- The 60/40 project/shared split and the category percentages are the default behavior shown in the docs. Enterprise plans support more advanced/custom collection configurations (e.g., custom categories).
- You can explicitly fetch shared context via rlm_shared_context (filter categories, set max_tokens) to inspect the allocation before running a full rlm_context_query.

Short answer
- When you query a project, Snipara automatically reserves part of your token budget for team shared context. By default the split is 60% project docs / 40% shared context.
- The 40% shared-context budget is then apportioned across the four document categories by rule: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.

How it works (details)
- rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=...) receives the full token budget you request. Snipara splits that budget: ~60% is used to retrieve project-specific sections; ~40% is reserved for shared collection documents.
- Within the shared-context slice, Snipara applies the category allocation:
  - MANDATORY → 40% of the shared slice
  - BEST_PRACTICES → 30%
  - GUIDELINES → 20%
  - REFERENCE → 10%
- Individual documents within a category are ranked using their priority (0–100) and relevance to your query; higher-priority documents get more of the category budget.
- You can call rlm_shared_context(...) directly (categories filter, max_tokens) to see the documents and the returned budget_allocation (the API returns total_tokens and per-category allocations).
- You can also inject shared context into the session (rlm_inject) so it applies to subsequent queries.

Example (6000-token query)
- rlm_context_query(..., max_tokens=6000) → project ≈ 3600 tokens, shared ≈ 2400 tokens.
- Shared 2400 → MANDATORY ≈ 960, BEST_PRACTICES ≈ 720, GUIDELINES ≈ 480, REFERENCE ≈ 240.

Notes / tips
- The category percentages are the configured defaults/priority order (MANDATORY highest).
- Document-level priority (0–100) further influences selection and allocation inside each category.
- You can request only particular categories via rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) if you want a different mix.

Short answer
- When you query a project, Snipara automatically reserves part of your token budget for shared (team) context and the rest for project docs. By default that split is 60% project docs / 40% shared context.
- The shared-context portion is then allocated across document categories using fixed weights:
  - MANDATORY = 40% of shared budget
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 30% of shared budget
  - GUIDELINES = 20% of shared budget
  - REFERENCE = 10% of shared budget

How it works in practice
- rlm_context_query(max_tokens=...) applies the 60/40 split automatically: e.g., max_tokens=6000 → 3600 tokens to project docs, 2400 to shared context.
- The 2400 shared tokens are split by category: MANDATORY 40% (960 tokens), BEST_PRACTICES 30% (720), GUIDELINES 20% (480), REFERENCE 10% (240). Snipara returns the highest-priority documents per category until each category’s budget is filled.
- Documents within a category are ordered by their priority (0–100) and truncated/omitted to fit the category budget.
- You can explicitly fetch shared context with rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...), which returns the documents plus a budget_allocation object showing exact token splits.
- Snipara will include shared context automatically in rlm_context_query results (token budgeting applied), or you can inject shared context into the session (rlm_inject) to make it apply to subsequent queries.

Notes and tips
- You can filter which categories to include when calling rlm_shared_context to control what gets budgeted.
- If a category has less content than its allocation, Snipara favors higher-priority docs; remaining budget is effectively unused or reallocated by the system to maximize relevance.
- Use rlm_shared_context first at session start (recommended) so team rules are consistently available to subsequent rlm_context_query calls.

Short answer
The docs do not describe any built‑in “real‑time collaborative editor” feature in Snipara. There’s no native Google‑Docs/Live‑collab editing service documented. To get real‑time collaboration, combine Snipara’s team/shared context + MCP tooling with a real‑time editor (VS Code Live Share, Codespaces, etc.). Below are recommended, documented steps and examples.

Recommended approach (documented features only)
1. Ensure team/enterprise plan
   - Shared Collections and multi‑project queries require a Team plan (see Shared Context / Access Levels).

2. Share team standards and context
   - Create a Shared Context Collection and upload team coding standards (rlm_upload_shared_document).
   - Link the collection to your projects in the dashboard so all project queries include team context automatically.

3. Give each developer MCP access
   - Add per‑project .mcp.json for each developer with the project MCP endpoint and an API key (team API key for cross‑project access).
   - Example .mcp.json snippet (from docs):
     {
       "mcpServers": {
         "snipara": {
           "type": "http",
           "url": "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara",
           "headers": {
             "X-API-Key": "<your-api-key>"
           }
         }
       }
     }

4. Use Snipara tools in sessions
   - At session start load team rules:
     shared = rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"])
     rlm_inject(context=f"Team Coding Standards:\n{shared['documents'][0]['content']}", append=False)
   - Developers can then run rlm_context_query, rlm_plan, rlm_decompose, etc., and all results will include shared context.

5. Add editor-level real-time collaboration
   - Use a real‑time code editor/collaboration tool (VS Code Live Share, GitHub Codespaces, JetBrains Code With Me, etc.) for simultaneous editing.
   - Install the Snipara VS Code extension in each collaborator’s editor so they can query MCP, use rlm tools, and view shared context while editing:
     - VS Code extension install: ext install snipara.snipara
   - Optionally install the Claude Code plugin for extra slash commands: /plugin marketplace add Snipara/snipara-claude

6. Workflow example (combine both)
   - Start Live Share session (or Codespace).
   - All participants have the same .mcp.json and valid API key.
   - Load team standards with rlm_shared_context → rlm_inject.
   - While editing together, run rlm_context_query for focused context and use RLM-Runtime for safe execution/testing per chunk.

Commands / snippets (documented)
- Install VS Code extension:
  ext install snipara.snipara
- Install Claude Code plugin (optional):
  /plugin marketplace add Snipara/snipara-claude
- Example: upload shared doc (docs snippet)
  rlm_upload_shared_document(
    collection_id="team_collection_id",
    title="TypeScript Coding Standards",
    content="...rules...",
    category="BEST_PRACTICES",
    priority=90
  )
- Example: load shared context:
  shared = rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"])
  rlm_inject(context=f"Team Coding Standards:\n{shared['documents'][0]['content']}", append=False)

Risks and caveats (from docs)
- There is no documented native real‑time editor in Snipara — you must use third‑party editor collaboration tools.
- Ensure MCP auth/permissions: use correct X-API-Key (team API key for shared access).
- MCP server health & quotas: heavy concurrent sessions + many context queries can hit rate limits; verify server with health checks (curl /health) and tools/list after deployments.
- Concurrency conflicts: real‑time editing still requires git discipline (merge conflicts, patch‑only rules). Follow “Patch‑Only” guidance: minimal file touches and avoid large refactors during collaborative sessions unless preapproved.

Short answer
- When you exceed your plan's monthly Queries/mo quota, further context-optimization queries are blocked and you'll receive a quota/exceeded error. You must either wait for the quota to reset at the next billing cycle or upgrade your plan to restore access.

What you can do
- Check current usage: call the MCP stats tool (example from docs)
  curl -s -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara" \
    -H "X-API-Key: rlm_..." \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"rlm_stats","arguments":{}}}'
- Upgrade plan: choose from FREE (100/mo), PRO (5,000/mo), TEAM (20,000/mo), ENTERPRISE (Unlimited) via the billing/dashboard.
- If you need a temporary overage or Enterprise terms, contact support/sales to arrange a custom plan or increase quota.

If you hit the limit in automation or CI, add guardrails (fail early, catch quota errors) and surface upgrade instructions to users.

If rlm_context_query (or another Snipara MCP tool) returns no relevant documents, Snipara will simply return an empty / low‑relevance context. It does not invent context — you get no useful context to pass to the client LLM. Recommended next steps:

- Confirm result vs error
  - If the MCP call errored (500, timeout): follow the documented fallback — use local Read/Grep/Glob tools and re-run Snipara later.
  - If the call succeeded but returned no sections: proceed with the steps below.

- Quick retries / widen the search
  - Broaden or rephrase the query (synonyms, higher-level terms).
  - Try rlm_ask for a quick, smaller-budget query.

- Try other Snipara tools
  - rlm_search for regex/keyword matches in known files.
  - rlm_multi_project_query to search across all team projects.
  - rlm_shared_context to include team standards (they may contain the answer).

- If documentation is missing
  - Upload the missing feature/spec with rlm_upload_document and re-run the query.
  - Or ask the author/owner to add the docs to the project or a shared collection.

- When all else fails
  - Use local file reads/grep/glob to find implementation details (allowed fallback).
  - Don’t pass an empty context to the client LLM expecting a correct implementation — either gather/upload context or perform the work locally.

Example commands (examples from docs):
- rlm_context_query(query="authentication implementation", max_tokens=6000)
- rlm_ask(query="authentication", max_tokens=2500)
- rlm_search(pattern="OAuth|token", path="src/**")
- rlm_multi_project_query(query="How is authentication implemented?", project_ids=[])
- rlm_upload_document(path="docs/features/oauth.md", content="<spec>")

In short: a successful Snipara call with no relevant docs returns empty context — broaden the query, try other MCP tools, include shared context, or upload the missing docs; if the MCP server errored, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob.

If Snipara finds no relevant documents, rlm_context_query will return no sections (effectively an empty/0-token result) — so you get no optimized context to pass to the client LLM.

Recommended next steps (from the docs):
- Broaden or rephrase the query and try again.
- Try the quick query rlm_ask (predictable token size).
- Use rlm_search for regex/keyword lookups or rlm_multi_project_query to search across all team projects.
- Pull team rules with rlm_shared_context (maybe the answer lives in shared standards).
- If you already know file paths, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob tools to inspect files directly.
- If the MCP call errored (500/timeout) — treat it as an outage and fall back to local tools; also verify MCP health, API key, and .mcp.json configuration.

These are the documented options for when Snipara returns nothing or when the MCP is unavailable.

If rlm_context_query returns no relevant sections, Snipara simply returns an empty/no-results context — you don’t get any optimized context to pass to the LLM. The recommended next steps are:

- Rephrase or broaden the query and retry rlm_context_query.
- Try the quick query rlm_ask (predictable ~2500 token budget) for a different retrieval signal.
- Use rlm_search for regex/keyword searches if you know patterns or filenames.
- Use rlm_multi_project_query to search across all team projects (if you suspect the implementation lives elsewhere).
- Check team standards with rlm_shared_context in case the answer is in shared docs.
- If a spec or docs are missing, upload them (rlm_upload_document) so future queries can find them.
- If Snipara is unavailable or you know the file path, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob tools (local file read is allowed and preferred in that case).

Also note: the documented workflow treats “Snipara error (500/timeout/etc.)” the same way — fall back to local tools.
